note: Fallback context shown when no specific ripple_effects.json playbook matches a headline — guarantees every item has some 'why this matters' context, but deliberately kept general/structural rather than framed as a specific historical claim (that distinction is what the UI's separate labeling communicates). Every statement here describes a well-known, uncontroversial structural relationship in how this category of news tends to matter, hedged with 'can'/'commonly' language, never framed as a prediction about any specific story. Keep in sync with os/global-intelligence-site/data/category_context.json.

categories.why_it_matters: Economic indicators like GDP, inflation, and employment data are among the primary inputs policymakers and investors use to gauge the health of an economy.
typical_channels: Central banks commonly weigh this kind of data heavily in interest rate decisions.
It can shift expectations for a currency's value against others.
It often feeds into corporate earnings forecasts and broader stock market sentiment.

categories.why_it_matters: Markets news reflects how investors are pricing risk and opportunity in real time — in stocks, bonds, commodities, and currencies.
typical_channels: Price moves can directly affect the value of retirement savings and pension funds.
Borrowing costs for governments, companies, and households commonly move with bond markets.
Sharp market swings have historically been associated with shifts in consumer and business confidence.

categories.why_it_matters: Domestic political developments — elections, legislation, leadership changes — set the policy backdrop that businesses and investors plan around.
typical_channels: New laws and regulations can directly change costs or opportunities for specific industries.
Political uncertainty has historically been associated with businesses delaying investment decisions until the picture clears.
Fiscal policy choices (spending, taxation) commonly ripple into economic growth and inflation over time.

categories.why_it_matters: Geopolitical developments — conflicts, alliances, diplomatic shifts — can disrupt trade routes, supply chains, and cross-border investment.
typical_channels: Sanctions and export controls can directly cut off trade in specific goods or with specific countries.
Security concerns along major energy or shipping routes commonly affect transport costs.
Investor risk appetite for an affected region or asset class can shift quickly.

categories.why_it_matters: Energy prices are a foundational input cost across almost every other industry, from transportation to manufacturing to agriculture.
typical_channels: Transportation and shipping costs commonly move with fuel prices.
Energy-intensive manufacturing (chemicals, steel, fertilizer) is directly exposed to energy cost swings.
Household utility bills and discretionary spending can be affected by sustained energy price changes.

categories.why_it_matters: Technology developments — AI advances, chip supply, platform regulation — shape which companies and countries hold a competitive edge in fast-growing industries.
typical_channels: Company valuations and competitive positioning can shift quickly around major product or regulatory news.
Industries that depend on a given technology (autos, healthcare, finance) are often affected downstream.
Regulatory decisions in one major market have sometimes influenced how companies operate globally.

categories.why_it_matters: Scientific and public health developments can change treatment costs, insurance considerations, and health behavior at a population scale.
typical_channels: Healthcare and pharmaceutical company valuations often respond to major clinical or regulatory news.
Public health policy and insurance costs can shift in response to new health guidance.
Consumer behavior around an affected product, food, or activity has historically changed following major findings.

categories.why_it_matters: Climate and weather events increasingly intersect with agriculture, insurance, energy demand, and infrastructure planning.
typical_channels: Crop yields and food prices can be affected by extreme weather in major growing regions.
Insurance and reinsurance costs in an affected region have historically risen after severe events.
Energy demand for heating or cooling, and infrastructure resilience spending, can shift with climate trends.

categories.why_it_matters: Company-level news — earnings, leadership, strategy — signals how individual firms and their industries are performing.
typical_channels: Stock price and investor sentiment for the company and its close competitors commonly react.
Suppliers and customers in the same value chain can be indirectly affected.
Employment and wage trends in the affected industry sometimes follow major company decisions.

categories.why_it_matters: Social and demographic trends shape long-run consumer demand, labor markets, and public policy priorities.
typical_channels: Shifting demand patterns can affect which products and services see growth or decline.
Labor market composition and wage trends often move with demographic and social change.
Policy debates — and eventually legislation — frequently follow significant social trends.

categories.why_it_matters: Travel and mobility news affects tourism-dependent economies and the transportation industry directly, and can ripple into related consumer spending.
typical_channels: Airline, hotel, and tourism company revenue commonly moves with travel demand and disruptions.
Local economies dependent on tourism can be significantly affected by shifts in visitor numbers.
Adjacent spending — retail, hospitality, dining — often follows changes in travel patterns.

categories.why_it_matters: Food and agriculture news affects one of the most essential and closely watched cost categories for households worldwide.
typical_channels: Grocery and restaurant prices commonly move with underlying crop and livestock costs.
Farm input costs (seed, fertilizer, fuel) directly affect what it costs to grow food.
Countries that rely on imported food have historically been more exposed to price swings than major producers.
